english: mischief Mil.; bar-čád (bar chad) ma tsúgs-par (ma tshugs par) without having played me a roguish trick Mil.; also in a moral sense: temptation; sin, trespass, bár-du (bar du) ɣčód-pa (gcod pa) to commit sin, to trespass Mil. — *bár-ta* (*bar ta*) W. cloth round the loins. -- bar-stóṅ (bar stong) Sch. empty space. — bár-do (bar do) 1. also bar-ma-do (bar ma do) the intermediate state between death and re-birth, of a shorter or longer duration (yet not of more than 40 days, ni f.); although on the one hand it is firmly believed, that the place of re-birth (whether a man, an animal, or a god etc. go forth from it), unalterably depends on the former course of life, yet in Thgr. the soul is urged and instructed to proceed at once into Nirwana to Buddha (inconsistently with the general dogma). bár-do (bar do) ɣcód-pa (gcod pa) Mil. is explained as putting off and preventing the intermediate state after death, as well as re-birth, by penitentiary exercises. 2. W.: hard, difficult; difficulty, — perh. Bunan. — bar-snáṅ (bar snang) (seldom bar-snáṅ-ba (bar snang ba)) atmospherical space; stéṅ-gi (steng gi) bar-snáṅ-la (bar snang la) in the heavens, in the air, frq.; bar-snáṅ-la (bar snang la) ˳pár (par) (a fragment of a blasted rock) flies up into the air; bar-snáṅ-du (bar snang du) or -la (-la) c. genit., the common word for over, goï (go'i) bar-náṅ-la (bar nang la), over (his) head. — *bár-pa* (*bar pa*) W. the middle one, e.g. of three brothers. — bar-bár-du (bar bar du) Ma. at intervals, from time to time, now and then; bar-bár-la (bar bar la) id.; *mā (ma) bar-bár-la* (*bar bar la*), at long intervals, seldom W. — bár-ma (bar ma) the middle one of three things Glr. — bar-mí (bar mi) mediator, intercessor, umpire. — *bar-tsód* (*bar tshod*) W. middling; *lúṅ-po (lung po) bar-tsód* (*bar tshod*) a moderate wind. — bar-mtsháms (bar mtshams), bar-tsáms (bar mtshams) interval (Sch.: room; leisure, convenience, comfort?). — bar-lág-pa (bar lag pa) = bar-mí (bar mi) Sch. — *bar-lhag* (*bar lhag*) gap, vacancy, deficiency W., *bar-lág (bar lag) káṅ-če* (*kang che*) to fill up a gap or vacancy, to supply a want, or deficiency.
W. a field or estate let to a person for the term of his life, for usufruct.

wool, bál-gyi (bal gyi) woolen, bal daṅ (bal dang) ldán-pa (ldan pa) woolly. Wdṅ.; bal séd-pa (bal sed pa) the first coarse plucking of wool, rmél-ba (rmel ba) the second, of the finer wool, siṅ-ba (sing ba) the third, of the finest W.; *bal táb-če* (*bal thab che*) to beat wool W.; lug- (lug-), ra- (ra-), rṅá-bal (rnga bal) sheep-wool, goat's and camel's hair; rás-bal (ras bal), śíṅ-bal (shing bal) cotton Cs.; śíṅ-bal (shing bal) prob. also the down on willow-blossoms Sch.; sríṅ-bal (sring bal) Wdṅ., Schr.: raw silk, yet perh. also cotton; čú-bal (chu bal) a kind of moss on stones in brooks Cs. — bal-skúd (bal skud) a woolen thread or yarn, worsted. bal-skyé (bal skye) Sch.: mould on fermented liquors. — bal-gláṅ (bal glang), Cs. also bál-gyi (bal gyi) glaṅ-po-čé (glang po che), a kind of elephant, for which sometimes incorr. and ambiguously bá-laṅ (ba lang) is used, Dzl. and elsewh. — bal-tér (bal ter) thin woolen cloth Cs.; *bal-˳dáb* (*bal 'dab*) W. tuft of wool, as is used for spinning. — bal-prúg (bal phrug) thick woolen cloth. — bal-yás (bal yas) Sch. wool-card (?)

bal(-po)-yúl (bal (po) yul) Nepal, frq. designated as rín-po-čéi (rin po che'i) glíṅ (gling), and as the favourite country of the Klu (klu), or serpent-demons; bál-po-pa (bal po pa), fem. bál-po-ma (bal po ma), bál-mo (bal mo) Glr., a Nepal man or woman; bal-nyíṅ (bal nying) C., (-snyíṅs) (-snyings) a Nepal rupee; bal-sráṅ (bal srang) Tar., Nepal pease.

I. v. bás-pa (bas pa). — II. instrum. of ba (ba); bas-bldágs (bas bldags), 'licked by a cow', n. of a disease combined with the sensation, as if the skin had been licked off by a cow, cow-itch, cow-pox Cs. (?) — III. v. pas (pas), where there is to be added: to say nothing of, much less, e.g. kroṅ (khrong) ˳dom ('dom) dgu-brgyá-bas (dgu brgya bas) brgya (brgya) yaṅ (yang) ˳bru ('bru) mi (mi) tub (thub), a well a hundred fathoms deep cannot be dug, to say nothing of 900 fathoms (much less one of 900 f.) Glr.

(cf. Pers., Hind. بس) Cs.: pf. of byéd-pa (byed pa) inst. of, byás-pa (byas pa) in the signification of 'done (with), settled'; bás-par (bas par) byéd-pa (byed pa), id. Sch.; in Bal. frq.: *bas* (*bas*), *byas* (*byas*), *bas-se* (*bas se*), or also byás-te (byas te) yód* (*yod*) it is finished, completed, ready, all right; *der (der) bas (bas)* that is all of it, nothing more is left Sch.; in bgyís-su (bgyis su) bás-kyis (bas kyis) after having been made, caused, occasioned Mil., it stands as a sign of the preterite, similar to zin (zin); or like zad (zad): mi (mi) ɣčíg-gi (gcig gi) smán-du (sman du) ma (ma) bás-kyis (bas kyis) not only for one man it serves as a medicine Dzl.; bas-mta (bas mtha') border-country Sch.
